periods.name: Colonial Brazil
periods.description: After Portugal claimed the territory in 1500, the region was gradually transformed from a trading outpost into a large plantation colony dependent on enslaved African labor. Over three centuries, settlement expanded far inland, the colonial economy grew around sugar and later gold, and periodic conflicts revealed the tensions of imperial rule.

events.description: A Portuguese fleet commanded by Pedro Álvares Cabral landed on the Brazilian coast and claimed the territory for the Crown.
In the first decades, Portugal limited its presence to coastal trading posts, exchanging goods for brazilwood with Indigenous peoples rather than establishing permanent settlements.

Concerned by French incursions along the coast, John III dispatched the first colonization expedition, led by Martim Afonso de Sousa, to establish permanent settlements and assert territorial control.
Portugal divided the territory into fifteen hereditary captaincies granted to private nobles, who bore the costs of colonization in exchange for land rights and administrative authority.
Most captaincies failed; only Pernambuco and São Vicente showed real viability.
Portugal replaced the failed captaincy system with a centralized royal government, founding Salvador as the colonial capital.
The first Jesuit missionaries arrived alongside the new administration, playing a major role in Indigenous relations for the next two centuries.
The Portuguese and Spanish Crowns were united under Philip II — the Iberian Union — exposing Brazil to Spain's enemies, particularly the Dutch.
The Iberian Union also enabled settlers to push territorial expansion far beyond the original Tordesillas boundaries.
Portugal restored its independence from Spain through the Restoration War.
Dutch forces, who had occupied the sugar-producing northeast since the 1630s, were definitively expelled, restoring full Portuguese control over the region.
The Treaty of Madrid replaced the Tordesillas boundary with a principle of effective occupation, formally recognizing the vast territory settlers had claimed inland.
Chief minister the Marquis of Pombal expelled the Jesuit order from all Portuguese territories, removing the missionaries who had administered large Indigenous populations.
The colonial capital moved from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro, reflecting the southward shift of economic weight following the gold boom in Minas Gerais.
The Inconfidência Mineira — a conspiracy in Minas Gerais led by intellectuals inspired by the American and French revolutions — was the first serious independence plot in Brazil.
The conspiracy was suppressed and its main leader, Tiradentes, was executed.
Napoleon's invasion of Portugal forced the entire royal court to flee to Brazil under British naval escort, making Rio de Janeiro the seat of the Portuguese Empire.
Brazilian ports were opened to international trade for the first time.

periods.name: United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves
periods.description: With the Portuguese court still resident in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil was elevated from colony to an equal constituent kingdom within the Portuguese realm. When the court returned to Lisbon and Portugal moved to restore colonial restrictions, separation became increasingly likely.

leaders.name: John VI
leaders.title: King of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves

events.description: Brazil was formally elevated to the status of a kingdom equal to Portugal, making any return to colonial subordination politically untenable for local elites.
A liberal revolution in Portugal demanded the king's return to Lisbon and sought to recolonize Brazil by dismantling the institutions created since 1808.
John VI returned to Lisbon, leaving his son Pedro as regent in Brazil.
The Portuguese legislature ordered Pedro to return to Europe and moved to strip Brazil of its institutions, convincing local elites that independence was preferable to recolonization.

periods.name: Empire of Brazil
periods.description: Brazil declared independence and established a constitutional monarchy under the House of Braganza. The empire lasted nearly seven decades, maintaining national unity while neighboring Spanish American nations fragmented, and ended with a military coup that established a republic.

events.description: Pedro I declared Brazil's independence from Portugal on September 7 — the 'Cry of Ipiranga' — and was crowned emperor.
Unlike former Spanish colonies, Brazil remained unified as a single state under a monarchical system.
Brazil adopted its first constitution, establishing a monarchical system with a distinctive fourth 'moderating power' that placed the emperor above the executive, legislative, and judicial branches.
The Confederation of the Equator, a republican separatist revolt in the northeastern provinces, was suppressed within months.
After a costly and inconclusive war against Argentina over the Cisplatine province, Brazil recognized the independence of Uruguay as a neutral buffer state brokered by Britain.
Facing military mutinies, popular protests, and loss of elite support, Pedro I abdicated in favor of his five-year-old son and returned to Europe.
With no adult emperor on the throne, Brazil was governed first by a provisional then by an elected three-person regency.
The Additional Act decentralized power by creating provincial assemblies and replacing the collective regency with a single elected regent.
The Cabanagem revolt erupted in the Amazon — one of the bloodiest in Brazilian history — reflecting deep social and regional tensions.
The Ragamuffin War broke out in Rio Grande do Sul, challenging the authority of the central government.
Unable to stabilize the country, Feijó resigned as regent.
The Balaiada revolt erupted in Maranhão — a social uprising of the poor, escaped enslaved people, and deserters against local oligarchies.
Parliament declared fourteen-year-old Pedro II legally of age to assume the throne, a political maneuver to end the instability of the regency and restore central authority.
The Eusébio de Queirós Law ended the transatlantic slave trade under British pressure, though slavery itself continued domestically for decades.
The Paraguayan War — the largest conflict in South American history — began after Paraguay attacked Brazil and Argentina.
The war ended, devastating Paraguay's population and leaving the Brazilian military stronger and politically assertive.
Princess Imperial Isabel signed the Golden Law, immediately abolishing slavery without compensation to slaveholders — the last country in the Western Hemisphere to do so.
The abolition alienated powerful landowners from the monarchy, fatally weakening Pedro II's political base.
A military coup led by Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca overthrew Pedro II, who chose not to resist and was exiled to Europe, ending sixty-seven years of imperial rule.

periods.name: First Brazilian Republic
periods.description: The republic inherited a federalist structure that concentrated power in the hands of regional agricultural elites, particularly those of São Paulo and Minas Gerais. The period was marked by political arrangements that excluded most of the population from meaningful participation, punctuated by military interventions and social conflicts.

events.description: The Republic was proclaimed on November 15 through a military coup, ending the monarchy without significant popular mobilization.
Brazil's first republican constitution was promulgated, establishing a federal system.
Deodoro dissolved Congress in an attempt to rule by decree, triggering a crisis that forced his resignation in favor of his vice president, Floriano Peixoto.
A naval revolt and a concurrent Federalist Revolution in the south challenged Peixoto's government.
Both conflicts were suppressed at significant human cost, paving the way for a transition to civilian rule.

Civilian elites from São Paulo and Minas Gerais took control of the federal government through a system of political bargaining known as 'café com leite' politics, excluding workers, the poor, and the military from power.

The War of Canudos ended with the destruction of a large religious community in the northeastern sertão. After three military expeditions were repelled, a fourth massacred the settlement, leaving around 25,000 dead.

Campos Sales formalized the 'política dos governadores,' a system of mutual support between the federal government and state oligarchies that guaranteed electoral results and consolidated the power of regional elites.
The Vaccine Revolt erupted in Rio de Janeiro after the government made smallpox vaccination compulsory, turning popular resistance into days of street fighting.
The government temporarily suspended the compulsory vaccination decree following the unrest.
Nilo Peçanha assumed the presidency after the death of Afonso Pena, becoming the first Afro-Brazilian president — a fact long silenced by official historiography.
The Revolt of the Lash was a mutiny by Black sailors of the Brazilian navy protesting the continued use of flogging — a punishment associated with slavery.
The government agreed to end the practice to defuse the revolt, but subsequently arrested and killed many of the mutineers.
After German submarines sank Brazilian merchant ships, Brazil declared war on Germany and the Central Powers, becoming the only South American country to participate militarily in World War I.
The Modern Art Week in São Paulo gathered writers, painters, and musicians who challenged European academic traditions to define a distinctly Brazilian cultural identity.
Junior military officers attempted a rebellion at the Copacabana Fort — one of a series of tenentista uprisings reflecting growing opposition to oligarchic rule.
A new tenentista revolt erupted in São Paulo and the south.
Part of the rebels formed the Prestes Column, which marched through the Brazilian interior for nearly three years attempting to mobilize the population against the government.
The global Great Depression caused coffee prices to collapse, devastating Brazil's export economy.
Washington Luís compounded the political crisis by nominating another São Paulo politician as his successor, fracturing the elite consensus with Minas Gerais that had held the republic together.
A military-backed revolution led by Getúlio Vargas overthrew the government, ending the Old Republic after forty-one years.

periods.name: Vargas Era
periods.description: Getúlio Vargas dominated Brazilian politics for fifteen years, first through a revolutionary provisional government, then through an elected presidency, and finally through a self-proclaimed dictatorship known as the Estado Novo. His government pursued rapid industrialization, created the framework of Brazilian labor law, and built a powerful centralized state, while suppressing political opposition.

events.description: São Paulo launched the Constitutionalist Revolution, an armed uprising demanding a return to constitutional government after two years of Vargas ruling by decree.
The revolt was suppressed after three months, though Vargas allowed a new constitution to be drafted as a concession.
A new constitution introduced women's suffrage and secret ballots for the first time in Brazil.
Vargas canceled upcoming elections, dissolved Congress, and imposed the Estado Novo — an authoritarian regime that concentrated all power in the executive and banned political parties.
After German submarines sank Brazilian ships, Brazil entered World War II on the Allied side and deployed some 25,000 soldiers to fight in Italy.
The Consolidation of Labor Laws (CLT) codified workers' rights including minimum wage, paid vacation, and regulated working hours, shaping Brazilian labor relations for decades.
Facing military pressure, Vargas was deposed by senior generals and sent into retirement, opening the way for democratic elections.

periods.name: Second Brazilian Republic
periods.description: Brazil's second democratic period began with competitive multiparty elections and a new constitution. It brought major urban and industrial growth, the construction of a new capital, and ambitious development programs, but also growing political polarization that ultimately ended with a military coup.

events.description: A new democratic constitution re-established direct elections, civil liberties, and a multiparty system after years of authoritarian rule.
The Brazilian Communist Party, which had won over 10% of votes in 1945, was banned under Cold War pressure and its elected representatives expelled.
Vargas returned to the presidency through a democratic election and resumed his nationalist development agenda.
Vargas created Petrobras as a state-owned oil company under the nationalist slogan 'O petróleo é nosso' (The oil is ours), driving state-led industrialization.
Facing a severe political crisis and military pressure for his resignation, Vargas took his own life — leaving a letter blaming foreign and domestic financial interests for his downfall.
Conservative military sectors tried to prevent the elected Juscelino Kubitschek from taking office.
A counter-coup by the War Minister removed acting president Carlos Luz after just three days; his successor Nereu Ramos guaranteed the transition and handed power to Kubitschek in January 1956.
Kubitschek launched his 'Fifty Years in Five' industrialization program, attracting foreign automakers and expanding infrastructure across the country.
The new federal capital Brasília was inaugurated in the interior after just four years of construction, symbolizing Brazil's modernizing ambitions.
Quadros resigned after just seven months in office — apparently hoping Congress would grant him broader powers.
Congress accepted the resignation, triggering a constitutional crisis over whether his left-leaning vice president, João Goulart, could take office over military objections.
To overcome military resistance, Congress temporarily adopted a parliamentary system that reduced presidential powers, allowing Goulart to take office as a constitutional compromise.
A national referendum restored full presidential powers to Goulart, who accelerated nationalist economic policies and social reforms, including controversial land reform proposals.

A military coup supported by conservative civilian groups and tacitly backed by the United States ousted Goulart, who fled into exile, ending democratic rule and inaugurating over two decades of military government.

periods.name: Military Dictatorship
periods.description: Five successive military presidents governed Brazil for twenty-one years. The regime suppressed political opposition, censored the press, and used systematic torture against dissidents. It also oversaw rapid economic growth in the late 1960s and early 1970s followed by economic decline, before initiating a controlled transition back to civilian rule.

events.description: Institutional Act No. 1 allowed the regime to strip citizens' political rights by executive decree and purge opponents from the civil service, military, and judiciary.
Mass student protests and strikes swept Brazilian cities.
The government responded with Institutional Act No. 5, suspending Congress, authorizing arbitrary arrest, and imposing censorship — marking the beginning of the harshest phase of the dictatorship.
The regime heavily promoted Brazil's FIFA World Cup victory to boost its image.
The economy grew at over 10% annually during the so-called 'economic miracle,' though gains were deeply unequal and dissent meant imprisonment or torture.
Geisel announced a policy of slow, gradual, and controlled political liberalization — a top-down process designed to keep the military in charge of the transition.

Journalist Vladimir Herzog was found dead in a military detention center in circumstances the regime claimed were suicide but were widely understood to be murder, provoking public outrage and accelerating pressure for liberalization.
Institutional Act No. 5 was revoked and an amnesty law freed political prisoners and allowed exiles to return.
The same amnesty law shielded military personnel from prosecution for human rights abuses — a controversial trade-off whose legal implications persist.
The Diretas Já ('Direct Elections Now') movement mobilized millions in the largest street protests in Brazilian history, demanding the right to vote directly for president.
The constitutional amendment for direct elections was rejected in Congress by a narrow margin.

The Electoral College elected opposition candidate Tancredo Neves, formally ending military rule. Neves fell gravely ill before his inauguration and died without taking office; his vice president José Sarney assumed the presidency.

periods.name: New Republic (Nova República)
periods.description: Brazil's ongoing democratic period began after the end of military rule. A new constitution enacted in 1988 established broad civil and social rights, and the country has since held regular competitive elections. The period has included economic stabilization, sustained poverty reduction, a major corruption crisis, and recurring political polarization.

events.description: The Cruzado Plan attempted to eliminate chronic hyperinflation through a freeze on prices and wages.
The plan collapsed within months as shortages emerged and inflation returned with greater force.

The Federal Constitution — known as the 'Citizen Constitution' — was promulgated, establishing direct elections, an independent judiciary, social rights to health and education, and protections for Indigenous and Afro-Brazilian communities.
Brazil held its first direct presidential election in twenty-nine years, won by Fernando Collor de Mello.
The Collor Plan froze private bank accounts above a set threshold to combat hyperinflation, disrupting the savings of millions of Brazilians overnight.
A congressional investigation revealed a broad influence-peddling scheme run by Collor's campaign treasurer.
Facing near-certain impeachment, Collor resigned; the Senate voted to impeach him regardless and suspended his political rights for eight years.
The Real Plan, developed by Finance Minister Fernando Henrique Cardoso, introduced a new currency and broke Brazil's cycle of hyperinflation.
Cardoso accelerated privatization of large state companies in telecommunications and energy, shifting Brazil toward market-oriented economic management.
A currency crisis forced Brazil to abandon its fixed exchange rate and float the real, requiring an IMF stabilization package.
Brazil recovered relatively quickly from the crisis, maintaining the macroeconomic framework inherited by the next government.
A global commodity boom strengthened Brazil's export economy as the new government turned to redistributive social policy.
The Bolsa Família program provided monthly payments to low-income families contingent on school attendance and vaccination, becoming one of the largest anti-poverty programs in the world.
Vast oil reserves were discovered in deepwater pre-salt layers off the coast of Rio de Janeiro, transforming Brazil's long-term energy prospects.
Brazil's economy grew 7.5% as Lula left office with approval ratings above 80%, after the largest reduction in poverty and inequality in the country's modern history.

Mass protests swept Brazilian cities, initially triggered by a bus fare increase in São Paulo and quickly broadened into discontent over public services, corruption, and the costs of hosting the 2014 World Cup and 2016 Olympics.

The Lava Jato (Car Wash) investigation uncovered a vast scheme in which construction companies paid bribes to Petrobras executives and politicians for inflated contracts, implicating figures across the political spectrum.
The commodity boom faded and Brazil entered a severe recession, deepening the political crisis around Rousseff's government.
The Senate voted to remove Rousseff on charges of manipulating budget accounts to conceal a fiscal deficit. Rousseff and her supporters maintained it was a politically motivated removal.
Congress approved a sweeping labor reform reducing protections established since the 1943 CLT.
A constitutional amendment capped public spending growth for twenty years, changes Temer framed as necessary to restore fiscal balance after the recession.
Deforestation in the Amazon increased sharply as the new government rolled back environmental enforcement, drawing sustained international criticism.
The COVID-19 pandemic killed over 700,000 Brazilians — the second-highest absolute death toll in the world.
The government's response was widely criticized for delays in vaccine procurement and the president's repeated dismissal of the disease's severity.
Lula defeated Bolsonaro in the presidential election's second round by less than two percentage points — the closest result in the history of the republic.

Days after Lula's inauguration, thousands of Bolsonaro supporters stormed and ransacked the Presidential Palace, Congress, and the Supreme Court — the most serious attack on Brazilian democratic institutions since the 1964 coup.

Lula returned to the presidency for a third non-consecutive term, focused on rebuilding environmental policy, reactivating social programs, and stabilizing democratic institutions after the January 8 attacks.
Brazil rejoined international climate commitments and reinstated environmental agencies dismantled under the previous government, pledging to eliminate illegal deforestation in the Amazon by 2030.
Brazil hosted the G20 summit in Rio de Janeiro, advancing proposals for a global minimum tax on billionaires and increased debt relief for developing countries.
